query: How well does this ACT policy generalize across manipulated-object properties, and where is its first likely weakness?

proposal.hypothesis: At the exact seed used by the blue appearance variant, restoring the target block to its clean appearance while preserving geometry and pose will restore ACT success and physical contact; if it does not, the observed appearance failure is not causally attributable to appearance and the clean-policy baseline is the more likely first weakness.

requested_perturbation.description: Run an exact-seed clean control for the appearance experiment by removing only the blue target-block appearance change and retaining the same task configuration, geometry, pose sampling, and seed 1010.
controlled_changes: restore only the target block appearance from blue to the official clean appearance

task_need.description: TaskGen must provide the official clean appearance control for the target block at exact seed 1010, using official passthrough or an equivalent scene that changes no property other than undoing the blue appearance variant.

tool_need.description: ToolGen must retrieve or generate an observable paired diagnostic reporting strict hammer-block physical contact, official success, minimum functional-point XY error, and the relevant ACT trajectory alignment for comparison with the blue run.

proposal.rationale: All tested property variants failed, but the post-budget clean anchor also failed, so current evidence cannot identify whether appearance is a causal weakness or merely coincides with a broader clean-scene baseline failure. An exact-seed clean control paired with the existing blue result is the most informative next experiment because it isolates appearance while improving contact observability and directly determines whether appearance is the first attributable manipulated-object weakness.
